one_line_takeaway: VidDoS is a white-box, train-once-deploy-anywhere universal adversarial patch attack that hijacks Video-LLM decoders into near-maximum-length generation via masked teacher forcing plus refusal/EOS-suppression losses, producing up to ~205x token expansion and ~15x+ latency inflation across three open Video-LLMs and three datasets, with a simulation showing this latency can breach human takeover safety margins in autonomous-driving streaming settings.
key_finding: Existing image-centric ELA baselines transfer poorly to Video-LLMs: Random Noise, Verbose Images and NICGSlowDown mostly produced token ratios near 1.0x and sometimes negative latency overhead (e.g., NICGSlowDown on BDDX/Qwen3-VL reached only a 2.6x token ratio), which the authors attribute to temporal aggregation/pooling acting as a low-pass filter that dilutes frame-wise perturbations. By contrast, VidDoS achieved massive degradation: on BDDX with Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct, Adv Tokens rose from a 2.00-token clean baseline to 394.60 (197.30x ratio) with latency inflating from 0.16s to 15.57s (15.41x overhead); on BDDX with Video-LLaVA-7B-hf, Adv Tokens reached 411.45 from a 2.00 baseline (205.73x ratio, 11.09x latency overhead) -- the paper's headline '>205x token expansion, >15x latency' figures. On VideoSimpleQA, Video-LLaVA was pushed from 30.65 to 462.55 tokens (17.9x, 11.6x latency overhead). Ablations showed: attack potency scales with patch size (48x48 -> 94.5 tokens/3.83s; 224x224 -> 307.8 tokens/11.92s); sparser temporal sampling (8 frames) counterintuitively amplified the effect (371.4 tokens) versus 16 frames (172.6 tokens); the replacement-patch parameterization was necessary (additive noise collapsed to 2.0 tokens); and all three loss terms contributed, with the full joint loss (172.6 tokens) outperforming L_TF-only (58.2 tokens) and ablated variants without L_ban (134.7) or L_stop (169.2). VidDoS also stayed robust to decoding temperature -- expansion ratio stayed above 240x even at T=1.5, and was slightly higher at T=0.7 (255.70x) than at T=0.0 greedy (243.62x). Cross-dataset transfer was strong between semantically similar driving domains (BDDX-trained patch on D2-City: 486.55 avg tokens) but degraded sharply across dissimilar domains (BDDX-trained patch on VideoSimpleQA: 63.00 tokens). The streaming safety simulation showed cumulative latency after attack-trigger injection climbing past 100s, far exceeding the tau_safe=2.72s human-takeover threshold, illustrating (in simulation) a critical-safety-violation consequence for autonomous-driving takeover scenarios.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is VidDoS, described as 'the first universal Energy-Latency Attack (ELA) framework specifically tailored for Video-LLMs.' It learns a single, spatially-concentrated, instance-agnostic replacement patch delta (via Sign-PGD, offline, on a surrogate/source dataset D_src) that is injected at a fixed peripheral pixel region (e.g., bottom-right corner) identically across all frames of any video. The patch is optimized against a novel tripartite loss: (1) Masked Teacher Forcing (a positionally-weighted cross-entropy loss over target 'sponge' tokens, applied only to the assistant response span), (2) a Refusal Penalty L_ban that suppresses the probability mass of a banned vocabulary ('Yes', 'No', EOS) at the first generation step, and (3) Early-Termination Suppression L_stop that suppresses EOS probability over a K-step prefix horizon. Once trained, the patch requires zero inference-time gradient computation and transfers 'train-once, deploy-anywhere' to unseen video streams.
threat_model: White-box Denial-of-Service / availability attack targeting deployed Video-LLMs in streaming contexts (e.g., autonomous-driving VQA). The adversary has full access to the target model's parameters theta to compute exact gradients during an OFFLINE optimization phase on a proxy/source dataset D_src (the target model itself is not queried at attack time). The learned universal trigger is then applied at inference time with no further optimization, no textual prompt modification, and bounded perturbation magnitude (patch region only, not an L-infinity-bounded additive perturbation over the whole frame -- the paper explicitly finds additive L-infinity noise 'almost completely fails' at 2.0 tokens vs. the replacement-patch parameterization). Attack goal: coerce the autoregressive decoder into an unbounded/near-maximum-length generation regime (L -> L_max) on normally closed-ended, concise queries, inflating GPU compute and end-to-end latency.
limitations: The threat model is white-box (full gradient access to the victim model's parameters) restricted to the offline patch-training phase; the paper does not evaluate black-box transfer to architecturally different, unseen Video-LLMs (only cross-dataset transfer on the same model is tested). Evaluation is limited to three open-source models at 4B-7B scale; no commercial/closed production Video-LLM deployment is attacked. The universal patch overfits to source-domain visual priors: transfer across dissimilar semantic domains (driving to general QA) drops attack potency substantially (63.00 tokens vs. 350+ in-domain). The autonomous-driving 'safety violation' analysis is a simulation built on a modeled streaming pipeline and a cumulative-latency equation, not a live on-vehicle test. No defenses or mitigations are proposed or evaluated -- the paper explicitly frames this as urging the community to 'recognize and mitigate' the threat, leaving countermeasures for future work.

datasets_used: VideoSimpleQA (reformatted to binary Yes/No verification task by the authors)
quantitative_results: BDDX / Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct: clean 2.00 tokens/0.16s -> VidDoS 394.60 tokens (197.30x ratio), 15.57s latency (15.41x overhead)
BDDX / Video-LLaVA-7B-hf: clean 2.00 tokens/0.38s -> VidDoS 411.45 tokens (205.73x ratio), 11.47s latency (11.09x overhead)
BDDX / LLaVA-NeXT-Video-7B: clean 28.40 tokens/1.12s -> VidDoS 333.50 tokens (9.38x ratio), 67.13s latency (8.25x overhead)
VideoSimpleQA / Video-LLaVA-7B-hf: clean 30.65 tokens/1.05s -> VidDoS 462.55 tokens (17.9x/12.67x ratio), 17.92s latency (11.62x overhead)
D2-City / Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct: clean 2.00 tokens/0.73s -> VidDoS 254.80 tokens (10.66x ratio), 127.40s latency (9.93x overhead)
Ablation - patch spatial size on BDDX/Qwen3-VL: 48x48 -> 94.5 tokens/3.83s overhead; 96x96 -> 172.6 tokens/6.74s; 224x224 -> 307.8 tokens/11.92s
Ablation - temporal frames: 8 (sparse) frames -> 371.4 tokens/14.71s; 16 frames -> 172.6 tokens/6.74s
Ablation - perturbation mode: additive L-infinity noise -> 2.0 tokens/0.09s; random-position patch -> 104.2 tokens/4.16s; fixed replacement patch (default) -> 172.6 tokens/6.74s
Ablation - loss components: L_TF only -> 58.2 tokens/2.26s; w/o L_ban -> 134.7 tokens/5.23s; w/o L_stop -> 169.2 tokens/6.63s; full joint loss -> 172.6 tokens/6.74s
Temperature robustness (Qwen3-VL/BDDX, patch trained at T=0.0): T=0.0 -> 487.25 tokens/19.21s/243.62x; T=0.7 -> 511.40 tokens/20.20s/255.70x; T=1.5 -> 511.45 tokens/20.55s/255.72x
Cross-dataset transfer (LLaVA-NeXT-Video-7B): BDDX-trained patch on D2-City -> 486.55 avg tokens; BDDX-trained patch on VideoSimpleQA -> 63.00 tokens
Streaming safety simulation: cumulative latency climbs to >100s after attack-trigger injection, exceeding safety threshold tau_safe = 2.72s (derived from cited human takeover-time meta-analysis)
baselines_compared: Verbose Images (image-VLM ELA adapted to video frames)
NICGSlowDown (image-captioning efficiency-degradation attack adapted to multi-frame video QA)
